summary: Dan Shapiro shares a DIY woodworking tip inspired by a wedding cake emergency. When his sister-in-law needed a pastry bag the day before his wedding, she improvised with Ziploc bags to frost the cake. Shapiro applies the same principle to woodworking: mixing sawdust from sanding with wood glue in a Ziploc bag creates 'Instant Liquid Wood' that can fill gaps and take stain like the surrounding material. The post is a lighthearted, early-blog-era maker tip that exemplifies Shapiro's tinkerer spirit.
key_points: Ziploc bags can substitute for pastry bags in a pinch — a trick that saved a wedding cake
Mixing 3 parts wood glue to 1 part sawdust creates a gap filler that matches the original wood and takes stain
The glue hardens in the bag tip, naturally sealing it for reuse
Cross-domain problem solving — applying a baking hack to woodworking — is a hallmark of maker thinking
money_quotes: You can turn the dust from your Random Orbital Sander of Eternal Happiness into instant liquid wood.
Just add about 3 parts Titebond (or other caesin glue) to one part sawdust and mix it all up in the bag.
Fill the gap high, since it shrinks when it dries, and sand it down when it's all the same homogenous color.
It even takes stain like the surrounding wood.
The best part is that the glue in the tip of the bag will harden, sealing it up for convenient reuse later.
key_insight: Creative cross-pollination between domains — borrowing a baking improvisation for woodworking — is the essence of maker ingenuity.
